label: human
author: Capri S. Wayne
topic: Film review of Heretic (2024) — Hugh Grant delivers but the ending falters
text:
“Heretic” functions as more than a typical horror film—it examines belief itself. Directors Scott Beck and Bryan Woods (“A Quiet Place” writers) tell of two Mormon missionaries encountering a mysterious man named Mr. Reed (Hugh Grant) at his isolated home on a rainy day.

The missionaries—Sister Paxton (Chloe East) and Sister Barnes (Sophie Thatcher)—enter Reed’s artificially warm living room for theological discussion. Cinematographer Chung-hoon Chung employs extreme closeups and 360-degree shots to intensify tension. Deliberate set details—a “Hubby” mug, a “bless this mess” wall quote—underscore the false comfort masking Reed’s invasive questioning.

Grant delivers a magnetic, unsettling performance, commanding attention during extended monologues. The dialogue balances humor with menace expertly. Both actresses, themselves ex-Mormons, bring authenticity through physical tension and measured politeness. East demonstrates particular range as Sister Paxton evolves throughout the film, while their relationship deepens audience investment.

A standout scene features Reed criticizing the sisters for not recognizing “The Air That I Breathe” by The Hollies. This moment metaphorically addresses his broader religious critique: how iterations and reinterpretations challenge singular truth claims. The house becomes purgatorial space where Reed questions “true religion”—a question the film deliberately leaves unresolved.

The first half masterfully blends dark comedy with psychological horror. However, the narrative falters once the sisters become genuinely trapped. Initial expectations of an elaborate maze game dissolve into single-room confinement. The final act, increasingly bloody and direct, abandons psychological suspense for conventional horror. Reed sheds his careful pretense, losing the intellectual sharpness characterizing earlier sections.

Despite uneven resolution, the film showcases strong performances and probes faith and control thoughtfully. Beck and Woods created a distinctly layered horror examination of belief’s fragility, though hampered by its disappointing conclusion.
